bug.title: APC inject does not protect the PID in the driver
bug.severity: On successful APC DLL injection the UI only updates local `protectedPids` state. It never calls `add_pid` / `StateAddPid`. The kernel therefore still treats the target process as unprotected: ObCallback handle monitoring and protected-PID thread/image notify paths never run for that process.

instructions.step: Preferred fix (kernel-side, no UI roundtrip): after a successful `DoInjectInContext` in `InjOnImageLoad` (when status is STATUS_SUCCESS), call `StateAddPid(ProcessId)`. Include `AppState.h` if not already visible from that translation unit.

Also call `StateAddPid` when injection is configured and the process is matched, if you want ObCallback protection even when injection fails — optional, decide consciously (protect-on-match vs protect-on-inject-success only).

UI hardening: in `+page.svelte` case `"apc_inject"` with `status === "success"`, call `await invoke("add_pid", { pid: d.pid })` in addition to updating `protectedPids`. Kernel-side alone is sufficient for ObCallback; UI still needs the list for display/filtering consistency.
On process exit, ensure `StateRemovePid` (or existing cleanup) drops the PID so the protected list cannot grow unbounded. Wire from `InjOnProcessExit` or process notify if not already present.
Do not only update frontend state — kernel `StateIsPidProtected` is the source of truth for ObCallback and notify filtering.

bug.verification: 1. Start driver + GUI, set injection target to `game.exe`, start `game.exe`. 2. Confirm `apc_inject` success in the log. 3. Without manually clicking "Add PID", run `cheat.exe` against the game PID. 4. Expect `ob_callback` / handle-access events. Before the fix these are missing. 5. Confirm UI "Protected" list includes the game PID.
bug.acceptance: After APC inject success, `StateIsPidProtected(pid)` is true in kernel without a separate userland `add_pid` command. ObCallback fires for external OpenProcess with dangerous rights on that PID.

bug.title: process_create / process_exit never emitted (dead API surface)
bug.severity: `CreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx` only drives injection via `InjOnProcessCreate` / `InjOnProcessExit`. It never builds or queues JSON events. Userland still defines `DriverEvent::ProcessCreate` / `ProcessExit` and the UI has empty case arms — dead end-to-end.

instructions.step: In `CreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx`, after injection handling, emit JSON via `ComsSendToUser` / `SendJsonString`: - Create: `{ "event": "process_create", "pid": N, "ppid": N, "image": "..." }` - Exit: `{ "event": "process_exit", "pid": N }` Obtain image name from `CreateInfo->ImageFileName` (ANSI conversion already used elsewhere). Parent PID: `CreateInfo->ParentProcessId` when available.
Escape image paths for JSON (see bug 9) — raw `\` in paths will break parsing.
Align field names with `driver_comm.rs` serde structs (`pid`, `ppid`, `image`). If you rename fields, update Rust and Svelte together.

Decide noise policy: emit for all processes vs only injection targets vs only protected PIDs. For process_create, "all" or "targets" is usually useful; for process_exit, at least protected + pending injection PIDs.
After emission works, fix bug 3 so userland does not drop these events if you want them in the UI. Or keep them for internal correlation only — document choice.

bug.verification: 1. Attach debugger or log kernel→user events while launching any process. 2. Confirm `process_create` JSON appears in the driver message queue / poll path. 3. Kill the process; confirm `process_exit`. 4. Parse successfully with `serde_json` / existing `recv_event` path.
bug.acceptance: Kernel emits valid `process_create` and `process_exit` events. Userland can parse them. No silent loss solely because the kernel never sent them.

bug.title: Kernel emits useful events; userland drops them
bug.severity: In `start_driver_polling` (`lib.rs`), events `process_create`, `process_exit`, `thread_create`, `thread_exit`, and `image_load` are matched and discarded. The UI still has handlers for `image_load` and empty cases for process/thread — so real-time shellcode (thread start address) and image-load telemetry never reach the frontend. Kernel work collecting start addresses is wasted.

instructions.step: Remove the blanket drop of `thread_create` and `image_load` from the poll match in `lib.rs`. Always emit those via `app.emit("driver-event", &obj)`.
For `process_create` / `process_exit`: emit once bug 2 is fixed. Until then keeping them dropped is harmless but document it.

Implement UI handling for `thread_create`: - If `start_address` is present, resolve or flag "outside known modules" when possible (even a simple log with pid/tid/start_address is enough initially). - Rate-limit image_load in UI (e.g. only non-system paths or first N per second) to avoid log spam.
Ensure JSON from kernel for thread events uses escaped fields if any path is added later (start_address is hex string today — fine).

bug.verification: 1. Protect a PID (or fix bug 1 so inject auto-protects). 2. Start a new thread in that process (e.g. `cheat_shellcode` remote thread). 3. Confirm `thread_create` with `start_address` appears in the GUI log. 4. Load a DLL into the process; confirm `image_load` appears (possibly filtered). 5. Confirm log does not become unusable from thread_exit spam.
bug.acceptance: `thread_create` (with start address when available) and `image_load` reach the UI. High-noise events are intentionally filtered, not accidentally all dropped.

bug.title: Overlay detection is a stub (README claims it works)
bug.severity: `detections/overlay.rs` returns an empty `Vec` with a TODO. No Tauri command exposes it. README Detection #10 documents working overlay detection — false.

instructions.step: Implement `detect_overlays()` properly using Win32: - `EnumWindows` callback - For each top-level visible window: `GetWindowLong(GWL_EXSTYLE)` for `WS_EX_LAYERED` / `WS_EX_TRANSPARENT` / `WS_EX_TOPMOST` - `GetWindowRect` + screen metrics: flag near-fullscreen overlays - `GetWindowThreadProcessId`, class name (`GetClassNameW`), title (`GetWindowTextW`) - Skip own process PID and obvious OS shells if needed
Fill `OverlayWindow { hwnd, class_name, title, pid }` for each hit. Prefer `windows` crate APIs already used elsewhere in the project.
Add Tauri command e.g. `scan_overlays() -> Vec<OverlayWindow>` in `lib.rs` and register it in `generate_handler![]`.
Add a UI button that invokes the command and logs each overlay with a clear tag (e.g. `overlay`).
If you intentionally leave it unimplemented, remove README claim and the dead module surface — do not leave false documentation. Prefer full implementation.

bug.verification: 1. Create a layered topmost fullscreen window (or use a known overlay tool). 2. Run scan from GUI; expect that window listed with correct PID/class. 3. Normal desktop apps should not all be flagged (tune heuristics).
bug.acceptance: Overlay scan returns real windows matching layered/transparent/topmost+fullscreen heuristics. Exposed via Tauri + UI. README matches behavior.

bug.title: Thread scan: GetThreadContext without SuspendThread
bug.severity: `check_all_threads` calls `GetThreadContext` on running threads without suspending them. Windows does not guarantee a valid context for a running thread; RIP-based shellcode detection is unreliable. HWBP code in the DLL correctly suspends first.

instructions.step: Open threads with `THREAD_SUSPEND_RESUME | THREAD_GET_CONTEXT | THREAD_QUERY_INFORMATION`.

For each target thread (skip the scanning thread if same process): `SuspendThread` → `GetThreadContext` → read RIP/DRx → `ResumeThread` in a deferred/cleanup path so resume always runs (even on early continue).
Handle suspend failures (access denied, already exiting): skip thread, do not panic; still try `NtQueryInformationThread` for start address without context.
Keep start-address checks independent of context success — start address does not need suspend.
Optional later: WoW64 / x86 CONTEXT — current struct is x64-only; document or fix if scanning 32-bit targets matters.

bug.verification: 1. Run `game.exe`, then `cheat_shellcode.exe` against it (remote thread outside modules). 2. Run thread scan; `suspicious` should be true for the shellcode thread with a meaningful RIP when the thread is still alive. 3. Confirm no hung game after scan (all suspended threads resumed).
bug.acceptance: Context is only read after successful suspend. Threads always resumed. Suspicious detection based on RIP/start address is reliable for live remote shellcode threads.

bug.title: clear_injection_targets does not clear targets

instructions.step: Add a new IOCTL command byte (e.g. case 14) in `Coms.c` that calls `InjClearTargets()` and optionally `InjSetEnabled(FALSE)`. Document the opcode next to existing cases 1–13.
Add `DriverHandle::clear_injection_targets()` in `driver_comm.rs` that sends that command byte.
Change Tauri `clear_injection_targets` to call the new command (clear list + disable), not only `set_injection_enabled(false)`.
Keep a separate "disable without clear" only if the UI needs it; otherwise one clear API is enough. Update UI button label if wording is wrong.
UI already zeros `injectionTargets` array — keep that in sync with kernel.

bug.verification: 1. Add target `game.exe`, clear targets, re-enable injection (if separate), start game. 2. Expect no APC inject. Before fix, inject may still fire if only disabled then re-enabled. 3. After clear, add a different target name; only that name should inject.
bug.acceptance: Clear removes all target names in kernel state and disables injection. Subsequent enables require re-adding targets.

bug.title: APC injection leaves permanent RWX private memory in the target
bug.severity: `DoInjectInContext` allocates shellcode + UNICODE_STRING with `PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE`, queues a user APC, and never frees or reprotects the region. A VAD scan of the protected process flags Peregrine's own page (self noise). RWX private memory is also a poor security posture for AC code.

file.note: Will report this region as vad_suspicious

instructions.step: Preferred layout: allocate two regions or one region with staged protection: - Write shellcode + path as RW - After writing, `ZwProtectVirtualMemory` shellcode portion to PAGE_EXECUTE_READ and path/ustr to PAGE_READWRITE or PAGE_READONLY Avoid long-lived PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE.

Freeing is harder: free only after LdrLoadDll has completed. Options: (A) Extended shellcode that VirtualFree's its own allocation after LdrLoadDll (needs resolved VirtualFree / NtFreeVirtualMemory address). (B) Kernel work item / delayed free after image load of PeregrineDLL is observed in `LoadImageNotifyRoutine`. (C) Leave allocation but RX-only and small; document as known residual and whitelist that base in VAD scan if you can identify it — least ideal. Prefer (A) or (B) for a robust design.
If implementing (B): on `image_load` of the AC DLL path in that PID, free the pending inject allocation recorded in a small per-PID map at inject time.
Ensure failure paths free the allocation if APC queue fails.
After fix, VAD scan of a freshly injected clean `game.exe` should not report Peregrine shellcode as suspicious (or only briefly before free).

bug.verification: 1. Inject into `game.exe`, wait until DLL hello arrives. 2. Run kernel VAD scan on game PID. 3. Expect no PE-less RWX private region from Peregrine (or region gone / RX only). 4. Confirm hooks still work (IPC hello + OpenProcess from cheat still reported).
bug.acceptance: No permanent RWX private page left by APC inject. Injection and hooks still succeed. Clean process VAD scan is not polluted by AC self-artifacts.

bug.title: Minifilter self-defense file names do not match real artifacts
bug.severity: `IsProtectedFile` matches `peregrine64.dll` / `peregrine32.dll` but build/install produces `PeregrineDLL_x64.dll` / `PeregrineDLL_x86.dll`. Those files are not covered. Minifilter only reports write/delete/rename; it does not deny. README implies file-access monitoring of AC files.

instructions.step: Update `IsProtectedFile` suffixes to match real names (case-insensitive ends-with): - `peregrinekernelcomponent.sys` (already) - `peregrinedll_x64.dll`, `peregrinedll_x86.dll` - `peregrine-tauri.exe` - Keep legacy `peregrine64.dll` / `peregrine32.dll` if still used as alternate search names in `connect_driver`.
Optionally protect `rules.yar` and install directory config if desired.

Decide report-only vs deny: - Report-only: keep current Pre* return `FLT_PREOP_SUCCESS_NO_CALLBACK` after log. - Deny: for non-protected callers, set `Data->IoStatus.Status = STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED` and return `FLT_PREOP_COMPLETE` for write/delete/rename of protected files. For educational AC, deny is more useful; document that protected PIDs still pass via `IsOurProcess()`.
Ensure `connect_driver` / install paths still place files under a path containing `\peregrine\` so `ContainsI(..., L"\\peregrine\\")` continues to match.

bug.verification: 1. From an unprotected process, attempt to open `C:\Peregrine\PeregrineDLL_x64.dll` for write/delete. 2. Expect `file_access` event (and ACCESS_DENIED if deny mode implemented). 3. Confirm AC process can still update its own files if that is intended.
bug.acceptance: Real installed AC filenames are covered by `IsProtectedFile`. Events fire for tampering attempts. Behavior matches README (report and/or block as chosen).

bug.title: JSON from kernel is not valid for many paths (brittle escape hack)
bug.severity: Kernel builds JSON with raw strings (paths with `\`, possible `"`). Userland does `s.replace('\\', "\\\\")` before `serde_json::from_str`. That is fragile: quotes still break, double-escaping risks, and unrelated fields can be corrupted. Events with paths (`image_load`, `file_access`, `process_create`, driver scan) can fail to parse silently.

instructions.step: Add a shared kernel helper, e.g. `JsonEscapeAndAppend(dest, destSize, src)` that escapes `\`, `"`, and control chars per JSON string rules. Use it for every untrusted/string field inserted into event JSON.
Alternatively emit paths as JSON strings built only through the helper, or use hex-encoded paths for simplicity (less readable but always safe).
Apply to at least: image paths, file_access paths, process image, driver paths, any user-controlled name fields.
Remove the userland global backslash replace hack once kernel emits valid JSON. Parse with `serde_json::from_str` directly; log parse failures with raw payload for debugging.
DLL IPC (`ipc_log_event`) has the same class of issue if image names ever contain quotes — escape there too for consistency.

bug.verification: 1. Trigger `image_load` / `file_access` with a normal Windows path containing `\`. 2. Confirm events parse and appear in the UI without the replace hack. 3. Unit-test or manual test: path with a double-quote character if creatable; must not break the parser or must be escaped.
bug.acceptance: All kernel→user JSON messages are strictly valid JSON without userland massaging. Path-bearing events reliably reach the frontend.

bug.title: Named pipe is world-writable; driver device lacks client ACL
bug.severity: IPC pipe SDDL is `D:(A;;GA;;;WD)` (Everyone full access). Any process can connect and forge hook/callstack events into the GUI. The Peregrine device is created with `IoCreateDevice` without a restrictive security descriptor; any user who can open `\\.\Peregrine` can send IOCTLs including set-PPL, add PIDs, configure injection.

instructions.step: Pipe ACL (minimum viable): - Allow full access to Local System and Administrators. - Allow the AC user / interactive logon session that runs the GUI. - Deny Everyone (`WD`) generic all. Example direction (tune to your deploy model): `D:(A;;GA;;;SY)(A;;GA;;;BA)(A;;GA;;;LA)` or add the specific user SID at runtime.
Better: create the pipe with a security descriptor that grants `FILE_GENERIC_WRITE` only to processes you trust. For educational AC, Admin+SYSTEM is often enough if GUI and targets run elevated.

Optional authenticity: DLL sends a shared secret or HMAC established at inject time (IOCTL-configured nonce written into target). GUI rejects events without valid token. Only add if you can do it cleanly — do not half-implement crypto.
Driver device: use `IoCreateDeviceSecure` (or set DACL after create) so only Administrators (or a dedicated SID) can open the device. Verify `FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN` remains set.
Document that the GUI must run elevated to open the device and pipe under the new ACLs. Update README usage if needed.

Confirm injected DLL can still open the pipe under the new ACL when running inside a medium-IL game — this may require granting write to a broader group or injecting a capability. If medium-IL games cannot open an Admin-only pipe, use a custom ACE for "same session" or a constrained SD that allows authenticated users write-only while Admin owns the server. Design this carefully; do not leave Everyone-GA as the solution.

bug.verification: 1. Non-admin low-privilege process attempts to open `\\.\pipe\peregrine_ipc` for write and `\\.\Peregrine` for IOCTL — should fail (or lack dangerous rights). 2. Elevated GUI still receives real DLL events after inject into `game.exe`. 3. Forged events from an unprivileged process no longer appear in the log.
bug.acceptance: Untrusted processes cannot forge IPC telemetry or issue driver commands. Legitimate GUI + injected DLL communication still works under the chosen security model (document IL/elevation requirements).

workflow.rationale: Fix auto-protect (1) first so other detections can be tested on a real protected game. Then un-drop events (3) and emit process events (2). JSON validity (9) unlocks reliable path-bearing telemetry. Thread scan (5) and APC RWX (7) are independent quality fixes. Clear targets (6) and minifilter names (8) are smaller. Overlay (4) is feature completion. ACL (10) last so functional testing stays easy during earlier work, then lock down.

do.item: Build kernel (.sys), DLL (x64/x86), Tauri GUI, and test/ binaries.
Produce a deploy package (folder or archive) with the exact files the VM needs.
Static review: compile clean, no unused IOCTL paths, README notes if needed.

do_not.item: Do not load PeregrineKernelComponent.sys on the DEV machine.
Do not run install.bat / sc create / test signing workflow for real tests on DEV.
Do not mark a bug status=done based only on a successful build.

test_vm.role: Only place where driver load, injection, ETW-TI/PPL, and cheat tests run.

prerequisites.item: Windows 10/11 x64 with test signing enabled (bcdedit /set testsigning on) if using unsigned .sys.
Admin shell for sc.exe, driver start, and elevated GUI.
Stable deploy path, typically C:\Peregrine\ (matches connect_driver search paths).
Snapshot the VM before risky kernel changes so a bad .sys is recoverable.

deploy.item: Copy from DEV build outputs to VM C:\Peregrine\ (overwrite previous bits): - PeregrineKernelComponent.sys - PeregrineDLL_x64.dll, PeregrineDLL_x86.dll - peregrine-tauri.exe (and any sidecar resources) - rules.yar - test\ game.exe, cheat*.exe, payload.dll as needed
If the service already exists: sc stop Peregrine, replace .sys, sc start Peregrine. If first install: follow README / install.bat (service + minifilter instance registry).
Confirm DLL paths the GUI will set point at the VM copy under C:\Peregrine\, not a leftover DEV path (connect_driver also searches C:\Peregrine).
Copy a matching set of binaries per change set — never mix an old .sys with a new GUI that expects new IOCTL opcodes (or vice versa).

verify_on_vm_only.item: Every <verification> block in this file runs on the VM after deploy.
Use VM Event Viewer / DbgView for KdPrint if diagnosing kernel failures.
Record pass/fail notes (optional) when flipping status to done.

deploy_checklist.item: Package: sys + both DLLs + GUI + rules.yar + needed test tools.
Transfer to VM (shared folder, scp, USB, etc.).
Stop driver if loaded; replace files; start driver; launch elevated GUI.
Run that bug's verification steps on the VM.
Only then set status=done in bugs.xml (on DEV, when you next edit the repo).

notes.item: Kernel bugs (1, 2, 6–10 partly) always need a VM driver reload cycle.
Pure userland bugs (3, 4, 5, parts of 9) still need the VM if they depend on live processes, hooks, or driver events — do not rely on DEV-only dry runs.
Hardcoded DEV paths (e.g. E:\Peregrine\...) in connect_driver / sigscan are DEV conveniences; on the VM prefer C:\Peregrine\ and ensure artifacts live there.
If a fix cannot be verified on the VM yet, leave status=todo or blocked with a short note — do not mark done.

build.item: DEV — GUI: cd src/peregrine-tauri && npx tauri build (or cargo build for backend checks).
DEV — Tests: rebuild test/ tools if cheats changed.
VM — Deploy package to C:\Peregrine\, sc stop/start Peregrine, run verification.
VM — Tests: game.exe + cheat_*.exe only on the test VM.

rules.rule: No hacks or temporary duct-tape that will break later.
Prefer fixing design (kernel source of truth) over UI-only patches.
Update README when behavior changes (especially overlay and file defense).
Mark status=done only after verification steps pass on the TEST VM, not after a DEV build alone.
Never load the experimental .sys on the machine that holds the source tree; use the dedicated VM.
Always deploy a consistent artifact set (sys + DLL + GUI) so IOCTL/IPC versions match.
